description: A/B test: CPython 3.12 vs Molt-compiled symphony hotspot _normalize_issue
methodology: Internal timing via time.perf_counter_ns(), excludes startup/shutdown

normalize_stripped.description: Full normalize_issue logic without dataclasses (50K iterations)

normalize_issue_full.description: Full normalize_issue with frozen dataclasses (50K iterations)
normalize_issue_full.note: Dataclass construction adds ~10x overhead on top of string ops

findings.baseline_overhead: Even pure arithmetic shows 11.4x regression on release profile, suggesting per-operation dispatch overhead in the runtime loop
findings.string_ops: String.strip()+lower() adds marginal overhead vs arithmetic (25x vs 11x), string intrinsics are working but not at parity
findings.dict_and_list: Dict lookups + list.append + list.sort in normalize_stripped compound to 45x, suggesting collection operations are a major contributor
findings.dataclasses: Frozen dataclass construction adds ~10x on top of everything else (398x vs 45x), likely going through slow call_bind path
findings.dev_vs_release: Release profile is 2-3x faster than dev across all kernels, consistent with opt-level and LTO differences

actionable_items: P0: Investigate per-operation dispatch overhead causing 11x baseline regression on arithmetic
P1: Optimize frozen dataclass construction path — bypass call_bind for known frozen fields
P1: Dict 'not in' check and list.sort() specialization for string-keyed small dicts
P2: String.strip()/lower() intrinsic path optimization — fuse strip+lower into single pass
P2: Profile with MOLT_PROFILE_JSON=1 to identify which runtime operations dominate
verdict: CPYTHON_FASTER on all kernels. Primary bottleneck is per-operation dispatch overhead (not string/collection intrinsics). Dataclass construction is a secondary 10x multiplier. These findings directly inform MOL-198 (hash merge), MOL-203 (deopt-driven recompilation), and the existing WASM performance gap.
